California
Fair POlitical
Practices Cornmission
July 2, 1991
Elmer A. Brown, President
E & L Associates
2110 K street, Suite 22
Sacramento, CA 9 5816
Re: Your Request- for
Informal, Assistance
Our File No. I -91-2 7 O
Dear Mr . Bror^In :
You have requested advice concerning.the campaign provisions
of the Pol'iticar Reform Act (the ngsgnl '1l 
Because your questions
are general j-n nature,. we are treating your request as one for
infoimal assistance. 2/
OUESTIONS
l-. How much rnay individuals contribute to candidates?
contributj-on limits apply separately to husbands and wives?
2. May businesses, including corporations, make
contr ibutions?
3. How much nay political action committees contribute to
cand idates ?
4. May cash contributions be rnade?
5. How should contribution checks be made out?
CONCLUSIONS AND ANALYSIS
1. On September 25, r9g1, the United States District court
for the Eastern District of california invalidated the state's
fiscal year contribution limits, which were added to the Act by
1/ 
covernment code Sections glooo-9101-5. AII statutory
references are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated'
Conrnission regulations appear at 2 california Code of Regulations
Section 18OOO; et seq. AtI references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations'
2l 
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
imrnunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice'
1Co".."i"^"t code se;tion gltrs; 2 cal- code of Regs' sections
^+?tt J Strrct, Suitc 8OO . P.C). Rox tit)7 O Sncrrnrcnto CA 95tt0^{-t'tstr7 a (() l6) IZZ-i6(r(r


page 2
Brown, Elmer
passage of Proposition 73 in 1988. (Service Emplovees
International Union, AFL-cIo. cl.c. et aI. v. Fair political
Practices Cornmission (L99O) 747 F. Supp. 58O. (t,SEIU',).)
However, contribution lirnits for state and local special elections
and special runoff elections were not affected by the court,s
decision and are stilL in effect. 
A I'special election't is an
election call-ed to fill 
a vacant state or Iocal office. 
(Section
85305.) In addition, contribution limits imposed by locat
jurisdictions 
may stiIl 
be applicable to candidates for locaI
office. 
Comnittees wishing to nake contributj.ons to candj.dates
for locaI office should contact the appropriate locaI jurisdiction
to ascertain if there are applicable l-oca1 Iirnits.
A person. inc)-uding an indj.vidual, proprietorship, firm,
partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company,
corporation or association, may contribute up to gl-,OOO per
candidate during the special eLection cycle and $1.OOO per
candidate during the.special runoff election cycle if a runoff
election is needed. {/ 
The contribution lirnits app}y separately to
each spouse. In addition, rnany cities and counties have imposed
Iower contribution Iimits on cindidates and comrnittees active in
their jurisdictions.
A person, including a corporation, which rnakes political
contributions totaling Sl-o,000 or more in a cal-endar year to
California state and local candidates and committees is also
required to file canpaign disclosure statenents for that year.
(Sections A2OL3 , 84200-84216. )
Under some circumstances, contributions nade by certain
combinations of contributors nust be aggregated. For example,
where a single individual in fact directs and controls the
contributions of an entity, both are treated as one contributor.
Most conmonly this witl be applicable where contributions are made
by a sole olrner or majority owner of a business entity and the
business entity itself. 
In addition, contributions made by two or
more entities must be aggregated if the same person or a najority
of the same persons in fact directs and controls the decisions of
the entities to make contributions. 
Business entities in a
parent-subsidiary relationship and business entities with the same
controlling owner are also considered one contributor, unless the
business e;tities 
act completely independently in their decisions
to make contributions. 
A parent-subsidiary relationship exists
3l 
The decision in sEru has been appealed. our advice i's
subject to change depending upon the outcone of this appeal'
4l 
"speciar eLection cycrerl
becomes vacant until the daY
runoff election cYcJ-err means
until the day of the. sPecial
means the day on vrhich the office
of the special election. 
"SpeciaI
the day after the sPecial election
runoff election. 
(Section 85305(b) .)


page 3
BrOWn′  Elmer
when one business entity owns more than 50 percent of another.
(Regulation l-8531.5, copy enclosed. )
2- State law does not prohibit corporate contributions.
3. Contributions nade by political 
cornmittees are not
subject to contribution Limits unless they are contributing to
candidates in a special election or special runoff election.
PoliticaI committees (a committee that receives contributions from
two or more persons) nay contribute up to $2,500 per candidate
during the special election cycfe and S2,5OO per candidate during
the special runoff el-ection cycle. (Section 85305(c) (2).) 
Broad
based political 
cornmittees (a comrnittee of persons which has been
in existence for more than six months, receives contributions from
one hundred or more persons, and nakes contributions to five or
rnore candidates) may contribute up to $5,ooo per candidate per
special election and $5,0OO per candidate per special runoff
election. 
(Section 8s3o5 (c) (3) . )
4. Contri.butions of $10O or more may not be nade or received
in cash. (Section 84300(a) . )
5. Section 8430O(c) requires that monetary contributions of
$100 or more be made I'by $rritten instrument containing the name of
the donor and the name of the payee.', A donor,s irnprinted check
made payable to a candidate,s canpaign committee would ordinarily
suff j-ce. In addition, Section 84211 requires candidates and
committees to disclose the fuII name, street address, occupation,
employer, date and amount received frorn each person who
contributes a cumulative amount of $1OO or more during a calendar
year.
There are many other provisi,ons of the Act r/hich affect
persons r4rho make or receive contributions or $/ho make expenditures
in connection with Cali.fornia state or local elections. 
Enclosed
are copies of the l-990 trlnformation Manuals on Carnpaign Disclosure
Provis j-ons of the Political Reform Actt and a trsummary of the 1991
Legislative Changes to the Carnpaign Provisions of the PoIiticaI
Ref orm Act. rr
If you have additional questions, please contact ne at (91G)
322-5662.
Sincerely,
Scott HaIlabrin
Acting ceneral cou.nse I
\
It
-'- 
l-\{Yl-lL - -.-- 
.).)- ( : -.
By: Wayne fP. Imberi
PoIiLlcaI Reforn consultant
Enclosures
